description: Strongly and solidly built; able to withstand rough use. 'Keruko' names the quality of sturdiness—construction that endures, form that resists damage.
sound_symbolism: The word stands like stone. 'Ke' begins with velar firmness, the decisive weight of something well-made. 'Ru' grounds with depth, roots reaching down into the earth. Then 'ko' closes with solid permanence, sturdiness confirmed. The hard consonants throughout mirror what sturdiness feels like: unyielding, reliable, built to last.
grammatical_notes: Functions as an adjective: 'keruko thae' (sturdy vessel). Opposite of 'welua' (fragile). Related to 'kema' (strong) but emphasizes resistance to damage rather than power.

pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Sturdy construction served generations—well-built tools and structures passed from parent to child.
pillars.solarpunk-values: Sturdy design reduces waste—things built to last don't need constant replacement.
